generated_comment: I’m all for supportive workplaces, but some of these policy conversations need more honesty. If a newsroom is already stretched, adding period leave and extended maternity benefits without thinking through staffing and cost does affect team planning, and it’s fair to ask why there’s no equivalent flexibility discussed for men. Otherwise it starts feeling less like inclusion and more like one side quietly carrying the workload.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s menstrual and maternity-related needs as a burden on the workplace and invokes reverse-discrimination logic about men. It encodes the ‘fair/unfair’ and economic/productivity surface manifestations in a polished professional register.
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 34. Married with one child aged three. A woman working as a journalist in Mumbai. You are on a panel at a media industry event. A male journalist on the same panel is asked about his biggest professional achievement this year. He describes a long investigation he published. He is not asked about his child. You are asked next about your biggest professional achievement this year. Before you can answer the moderator adds Ñ and of course as a working mother how do you balance it all. You are responding to this question in front of 300 people. What you say and what you do not say and the smile you maintain while saying it is the entire content of this persona.
